skill.name: lark-calendar
skill.description: Manage Lark Calendar schedules through lark-cli. The skill can review agendas, create events, check free-busy status, and suggest meeting times.
skill.summary: Review agendas, create events, check availability, and suggest meeting times in Lark Calendar.
skill.author: larksuite

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found most are markdown command examples, documentation links, Chinese text, and structured calendar examples rather than executable malware. The skill remains medium risk because it directs the agent to run lark-cli, perform networked Lark Calendar operations, create or delete events, and read a sibling shared skill file.

medium_findings.title: External CLI Can Modify Calendar Data
medium_findings.description: The skill requires lark-cli and instructs the agent to create events, add attendees, and use calendar API commands. This is legitimate for a Lark Calendar skill, but it can change user calendar data and should require clear user confirmation before writes.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines clearly document calendar creation commands and a caution that creation is a write operation. The behavior matches the skill purpose, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

medium_findings.title: Networked Lark API Access Requires Scoped Credentials
medium_findings.description: The skill documents calendar API resources and permission scopes for reading, creating, updating, and deleting calendar data. This is expected functionality, but it means the skill depends on authenticated network access to sensitive scheduling data.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation lists API commands and explicit Lark calendar scopes. I found no evidence of exfiltration to unrelated endpoints, but authenticated calendar access is sensitive.

medium_findings.title: Instruction Encourages External Tools for Date Conversion
medium_findings.description: The skill tells the agent to use system commands or script code for date and timestamp conversion. This can be safe when implemented with fixed tools and sanitized inputs, but it increases command execution risk if user-provided date text is interpolated into shell commands.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The instruction explicitly requires external tooling, but it does not provide an unsafe shell snippet. The risk depends on how the agent constructs conversion commands at runtime.

low_findings.title: Sibling File Read Dependency
low_findings.description: The skill instructs the agent to read a sibling lark-shared skill through parent-directory links. This appears to be a shared documentation dependency, but marketplace packaging should verify the referenced shared skill is reviewed and present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The parent-directory references are visible and repeated. They are markdown instructions rather than path traversal code, so this is a packaging and trust-boundary concern.

low_findings.title: Static Obfuscation and Weak Crypto Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The high-entropy and weak-crypto alerts appear to be caused by Chinese prose, dense examples, identifiers, and the word description rather than encoded payloads or cryptographic implementation. No executable obfuscated payload was found in the reviewed markdown files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found readable markdown and examples at the flagged locations. I did not find evidence of encrypted code, packed binary content, or a weak cryptographic algorithm implementation.

low_findings.title: Command Examples Triggered Static Shell Alerts
low_findings.description: Most external command detections are fenced lark-cli examples or option tables in markdown. They are not Ruby backtick execution and do not execute automatically when the skill is installed.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged content is inside markdown code fences showing intended lark-cli usage. There is no Ruby code, shell backtick operator, or automatic execution hook in those files.

dangerous_patterns.title: Calendar Write Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: Documented commands can create events, add attendees, and delete rollback events through lark-cli. These actions should only run after the user confirms the schedule details.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands and caution text directly describe write and delete operations. They are expected for this skill but can affect user data.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Date Conversion Requirement
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill mandates system commands or script code for date conversion. Implementations should avoid shell interpolation and prefer fixed argument arrays or trusted date libraries.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The risky instruction is explicit, but no unsafe conversion command is included. The runtime agent behavior determines the final exposure.

dangerous_patterns.title: Cross-Skill Read Instruction
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill asks the agent to read a sibling shared skill before use. This should be treated as a dependency on separately reviewed instructions, not as evidence of malicious path traversal.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The path is a real parent-directory reference in a mandatory instruction. It appears intentional for shared Lark authentication guidance, but it expands the review boundary.

content.value_statement: Coordinating meetings in Lark Calendar often requires checking agendas, availability, and attendee details across multiple commands. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through lark-cli workflows for safer scheduling and clearer meeting options.
actual_capabilities: Review today or upcoming Lark Calendar agenda entries.
Create calendar events with summaries, times, and attendee IDs.
Check a user primary calendar free-busy status for a time range.
Suggest multiple meeting time options for uncertain scheduling requests.
Use documented lark-cli calendar shortcuts before lower-level API commands.
Identify required Lark calendar permission scopes for supported operations.
limitations: Requires lark-cli authentication and the correct Lark calendar scopes.
Does not book meeting rooms unless a valid room ID is already available.
Free-busy checks only cover the queried primary calendar data.
Write operations can change real calendar data and need user confirmation.

use_cases.title: Plan a team meeting
use_cases.description: Find available times for attendees, present options, and create the selected Lark Calendar event.

use_cases.title: Review daily commitments
use_cases.description: Summarize today or this week agenda items in a readable timeline.

use_cases.title: Coordinate executive availability
use_cases.description: Check busy periods, avoid conflicts, and prepare confirmed meeting details before event creation.

prompt_templates.title: Show my agenda
prompt_templates.prompt: Show my Lark Calendar agenda for today in chronological order.

prompt_templates.title: Check availability
prompt_templates.prompt: Check whether user ou_example is free tomorrow from 10:00 to 12:00.

prompt_templates.title: Create a confirmed meeting
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a 30 minute product sync tomorrow at 15:00 with attendees ou_alice and ou_bob after confirming the details with me.

prompt_templates.title: Recommend meeting times
prompt_templates.prompt: Find three 45 minute options this week for ou_alice, ou_bob, and oc_team. Exclude lunch hours and explain any conflicts.

output_examples.output: A chronological agenda grouped by date, with each event time, title, duration, busy status, and RSVP status.

output_examples.input: Find a time tomorrow afternoon for Alice and Bob.
output_examples.output: A short list of recommended time slots with reasons, conflict notes, and a question asking which option to use.

output_examples.input: Schedule a design review at 3 PM with the product group.
output_examples.output: A confirmation request that includes the title, start time, end time, attendee IDs, and calendar write warning.

best_practices: Confirm the title, time, duration, and attendees before creating or changing an event.
Use free-busy or suggestion workflows before scheduling when the time is uncertain.
Use dry-run or schema checks before advanced API commands with custom data.
anti_patterns: Do not create events from ambiguous time requests without asking a clarifying question.
Do not assume an attendee identity when search results are not unique.
Do not expose detailed agenda contents when a free-busy answer is sufficient.

faq.question: Does this skill create real calendar events?
faq.answer: Yes. It can use lark-cli to create events when authenticated and authorized.

faq.question: Can it suggest meeting times?
faq.answer: Yes. It can request recommended time slots for uncertain scheduling requests.

faq.question: Does it require Lark credentials?
faq.answer: Yes. lark-cli must be configured with valid credentials and calendar scopes.

faq.question: Can it manage meeting rooms?
faq.answer: Only when a valid meeting room ID is already available in context.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: It is publishable with a medium-risk warning because it can run lark-cli and modify calendar data.

faq.question: What should users confirm before scheduling?
faq.answer: Users should confirm the event title, start time, end time, attendees, and calendar target.
